In requesting reconsideration of the advice provided, . you ask

b. Other FinanciaL lnterests
In addition to the above real property interests,

1. During 1989, wirtz Tile conpany, Inc. lras granted author-
ity to draw upon a line of credit held in the name of Mr. van

against said Iine of credit was approxinateLy S75,000.00 during

interest in the wirtz business is solely beneficial and as
security for said draw. The total consideration paid to Mr. van
Deventer fron Wirtz Tile company, Inc. uas 52,000. wirtz TiIe
conpany, Inc. is Iocated at 1533 Wilson Avenue, National city,
within the redevelopnent project area.
2. Mr. Van Deventer is the beneficiary of a note erith a cur-
rent ba.Lance of approximately S5,000 secured by a second deed of

under the note.
3. Mr. van Deventer is the obligee under a prornissory note
in the amount of $52,178.56 executed by Nancy Thompson, owner of
three child care facilities 
in National City. 
The child care
facilities 
are located at 2207 Highland Avenue, 22oo tt Ett Avenue,
and 1540 Harbison Avenue, National City. 
The Hightand Avenue and
rrEtt Avenue locati.ons are wi.thin the boundaries of the redevelop-
nent project area. The Harbison Avenue location is outside the

proximately 53,5oo in payrnent of this note in 1990-
4. 
1,1r. van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory note
in the anount of S11,889.55 executed by Eugenio Trinidad, also a
Iessee of Mr. van Deventer's and the oPerator of an auto electric
repair business at 115 East l,7th Street, Suite C, National city'
This location is within the redevelopment project area. This note

Additional Information Reqardinq National citv
You have requested information from the city planning and
finance departnents as to the number of owners of commercial real
estate and business interests, as well as residential properties,


our File No. I- 9o-559a
Page 6
rrithin the redevelopment project area. The findings of these
departnents are as fol1ows.
L. Based upon a survey completed at your request by the
National city Planning Departnent, it was determined that there
are 7L2 conmercial and industrial buildings in the redevelopment
project area.
2. A plannj.ng departnent survey of residential structures
indicates that there are 6,776 single-fa:niIy residences and 8,280
nuttiple-fanrily residences wj.thin National City. 
It is estinated
that ipproximately 4,525 single-fanily residences and 5,550 nulti-
fanily units are within the redevelopnent project area.
3- In order to further substantiate the number of business
interests within the redeveloprnent proiect area, you requested a
study frorn the National city Finance Department on the nunber of
businesses with sales tax pernits within the redevelopnent project
area. The finance department has determined that there are 1,782
businesses with sales tax permits Iocated in the project area.
This nurnber represents 78 percent of aIl' businesses with sales tax
permits in the city.
ANALYS I S
we have analyzed the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act which are applicable to the financial interests of Mayor
waters and councilmember van Deventer in our advice to you dated
April 15, 1991. (See Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-91-098. ) Please
rlfer to our discussion in that advice as it is equal'ly applicable
to these facts.
we proceed nol, to deternine rrhether the additional facts
provided with your request for reconsideration would alter our
advice to you dated October 5, 1990. (See Eiser Advice Letter,
No. I-90-559) we had previously advised you that the business
tenants leasing space in the cornroercial buildings owned by uayor
waters and Councilmenber van Deventer lrere potentially
disqualifying sources of income. we had also advised you that the
business interests of Mayor waters and Councilnember van Deventer
Iocated in the redevelopment project area e/ere potentially
disquatifying. 
Upon review of the additional inforrnation you have
provided, we now conclude .that the 'tpublic generally" exception
applies to these tenants and business interests which are,
therefore, not dj.squalifying financial interests. 
Holrever, the
ttpubLic generallytr exception does not apply to the cornrnercial real
estate interests of !,layor waters and councilnember van Deventer.
Moreover, the rrpublic generally" exception does not apply to
CounciLmember zarate's interest in an apartment building.
In addition, we are now providing you with a discussion of
the concept of bifurcation of issues which nay be applicable to
the proposed decisions. This issue was not raised in your
previous request for advice.


Our File No. I- 90-559a
Page 7
PubIic cenera I Iv
Even rrhen the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disqua I if ication is required only if the
effect of the decision is distinguishable from the effect on the
public Aenerally. (section 87103.) If the decision does not af-
fect al.1 mernbers of the public in the sane manner, disqualifica-
tion rnay be required unless the effect of the decision is the same
as its effect on a significant segTEent of the public.
(Regulation 18703.) The Conmission has never adopted a strict
arithrnetic test for deterrnining rrhat constitutes a significant
segTnent of the public. 
However, in order for the "public
generally" exception to appJ.y, the population affected nust be
large in number and heterogeneous in nature. (In re Ferraro
(1978) 4 FPPC ops. 62; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. I-88-430.)
Councilnember Zarate
Applying this analysis to your facts, lre conclude that the
"public generalLy't exception applies to councilmember zarate's
interest in her single-family residence. This residence is
Iocated within the redevelopnent area. You have advised us that
67 percent of aII single-family residences in the city are located
in the redevelopment area. Thus, any decision affectinq the value
of Councilmember zarate's residence wiII affect a significant seg-
ment of the public in National city in substantj.ally the same man-
ner. Accordingly, the "public Aenerally" exception appli'es to
this interest in real proPerty and therefore the real property is
not a disqualifying financial interest.
conversely, CounciLmenber Zarate's interest in an apartment
building is a disqualifying financial interest. 
Although 67
percent of aII apartnent buildings in National' City are located
wittrin the redevelopnent area, the t'public generallyrr exception is
inapplicable to this interest in real Property. The commission
has recognized a very narrow exception with respect to owners of
three or fe$/er rental units. 
In Ferraro, suDra, the Comrnission
stated that a group that was Iarge in nunbers and heterogeneous in
qua)-ity constituted a significant segnent of the public for
purposes of the "public generallyrt excepti.on. Applying these
principles, the Comrnission concluded that or.rners of three or feser
residential rental units in the city of Los AngeLes constituted a
significant segment of the public.
counciLnember Zarate's building consists of more than three
units. 
Thus, as the owner of nore than three rental unj.ts, she i,s
a nember of the residential rental property business shj.ch
constitutes an industry. In order to prevent members of
homogeneous interest groups fron participating in decisions which
wiII affect their own interests, the "public generally" exception
is inapplicable to nenbers of a single industry. 
(Ferraro, $PE!! r
at pages 64-66. ) 
This interest in real property is, therefore, a
disqualifyj.ng financial interest and councilnember zarate nay not
participate in decisions which will have a rnaterial financial
effect upon the value of this apartment buiJ.ding.


Our FiIe No. I- 9O-559a
Page 8 
.,
llavor Waters
a. Commercial Buildings
Mayor waters owns three conmercial buildings in the
redevelopnent area. There are 712 commercial and industrj,al
buildings within the redevelopnent area. Mayor waters leases
space in these buildings to a variety of retail merchants. There
are 1,782 businesses with sal,es tax pernits within the redevelop-
nent project area. This constitutes 78 percent of all the busi-
nesses with sales tax pernits operating in the city. 
This nunber
presurnably includes the co:nmercial office machines sales and
service business of r,rhich uayor waters is the proprietor and which
operates in one of these commercial buildings.
we had previously advised you that the business tenants in
these conmercial buildings r.rere disqualifying sources of j.ncome.
Hov/ever, you have provided us with the additional information that
78 percent of all businesses in the city are located hrithin the
redevelopnent area. For purposes of our discussion we assume that
a high percentage of these businesses are retail establishments.
Therefore, we no$, conclude that the retail merchants leasing space
in Mayor waters' conmercial buiJ.dings constitute the I'public
generally" for purposes of our a4alysis and are thus not
disqualifying sources of income.4 Note, however, that if a
particular decision will have a un.ique effect uPon one of these
tenants. d i sti,ngu ishable from the effect of the decision on other
retail merchants, disqua 1i f ication is required if the effect will
be :naterial.
In the context of redevelopment decisions, the Commission has
applied the ttpublic generally'r exception to retail businesses
located within the redevelopnent project area. (In re owen (1976)
2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In Owen, the Conmission concluded that retail
merchants within the redevelopment area constitute a najor part of
what is generally regarded as the business connunity which, taken
as a whole, may reasonably be regarded as the "Pub1j.c generally.rl
In owen, supra, the connission discussed the interests of
retail nerchants in the context of redevelopment decisions- The
Conrnission concluded that the adoption of the redeveloPnent plan
coul.d have some economic repercussions on the retail nerchants of
the city. 
However, when a substantial number and variety of
retaiL establishrnents wiLl be affected in substantially the sane
nanner, the "public generally't exception applies to these retail
nerchants.
4 wh"r, rendering ad.vice, the commission does not act as a finder
of fact, but bases its advice upon the information provi-ded by the
requester. (In re Oqlesbv (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) 
The
di.f ferent conclusion reached in this letter does not reflect 
a
change in legal interpretation, but only the application of legal
priniiples to the new facts lrhich you have provided-


Our File No. I- 90-559a
Page 9
The tenants occupying uayor waters' conmercial buildings_are
retail nerchants offering a wide range of goods and services.5
They include a money exchange shich buys and sells foreign cur-
rency and a cornmercial office nachines sales and service business
of which lIayor waters is the proprietor. 
These retail merchants
constitute a part of the business community in National city uhich
may be regarded as the rrpublic aenerally. " (Qre!, supra, at page
82., Accordingly, the tenants in these cornrnercial buildings are
not disqualifying sources of incorae.
Please note, however, that the conmerciaL buildings owned by
llayor waters continue to be disqualifying interests in real
property. This is so because, as ue have previously advised, you
have not supplied any facts that lrould indicate that a significant
segment of the public owns contnercial buildings within the
redevelopnent area. Thus, the |tpublic aenerally" exception does
not apply to l,layor waters' interests in conrmercial real estate.
b. RentaL Single-Fanily Unit
l1r. i{aters owns a sing),e-fanily residence within the
redevelopnent area which is rented to a tenant. Follouing the
analysis in Ferraro, supra, we conclude that the "public gener-
alIy" exception applies to this interest in real property which is
not a disqualifying financial interest.
Please note, however, that the tenant vrho leases the
residence is a source of incone to Mayor waters. consequently,
although the residence is not a disqualifying financial interest,
Mayor waters may not participate in any decision which will have a
:naterial financial effect on this tenant. The appropriate
standard for determining rnateriality t/ith respect to a source of
income who j.s an individual. indirectly invoJ.ved in a decision is
that of Regulation L8702.6 which states:
The effect of a decision is naterial as to an
individual who is a source of incone or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision wiIl affect the individual's
income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities 
(other than real
property) by $1,000 or nore; or
(b) The decision wiII affect the individual's
real property interest in a nanner that is
considered :naterial- under section 18702.3 or Sec-
tion 18702.4.
5  As diSCussed in our previous advice letter to you (Eiser′  No.
A-9■-098)′  theSe tenants are sources of income to Mayor Waters and
therefore they are potentially disqualifying sources Of income
pursuant to Sections 87■00 and 87■03(c).


our FiLe No. I- 90-559a
Page 10
Regulation LA7O2.6.
we have no infornation regarding this tenant and therefore rre
are unable to apply the standards of Regulation 18702.6 to this
individual,. 
Hor./ever, if you deternine that this tenant will' be
affected rnaterially by the pending redevelopment decisions, Mayor
waters rnust disqualify himself froro participating in such
decisions.
councilmember van Deventer
a. Comrnercial Buildings
Councilnernber van Deventer owns eight cornmercial buildinqs in
the redevelopnent area. Councilneurber Van Deventer leases space
in these buildings to a variety of retail merchants and to a
-orporation or wficn he is the majority stockholder.6 As
dislussed above !rith reference to !'tayor waters, retail nerchants
who lease space in Councilnember van Deventer's buil-dings are not
disqualifying sources of incone. These retail merchants provide a
variety oi gooas and services and include a retailer of auto fin-
ish products, an auto parts store, a party rental business, an
auto body and repair business. a weldinq shop, two truck and
automotive brake repair businesses, an auto glass service and
repair business, an auto electric repair business, an upholstery
snop, a tax service, and a wholesaler of cleaning solutj.ons.
Decisions affecting these retail nerchants wiIl also affect a1L
other retail merchants $/ithin the redevelopment area in
substantially the sane manner. (owen, suDra. ) Accordingly, the
t'publ j.c geneiallyf excePtion applies to these sources of j'ncome
which are not disqualifY ing.
Please note, hosever, that whereas the tenants in these
buildings constitute the ttpublic generally" for Purposes of our
analysia and are thus not disqual.ifying sources of incone, the
buildings do constitute a disqualifying interest in real property'
Additionally, as stated above, if a particular decision will have
a unique efiect upon one of these tenants, distinguishable frorn
the effect of the decision on other retail nerchants,
disqual i f icat ion is required if the effect will be naterial'
b. outstanding Loans
Mr. van Deventer has made certain loans to retail merchants
in the redevel.opment area. The loans are sti1l outstanding' As
we have previouily advised, these parties are sources of promised
incone to ltr. van Deventer. (Eiser, suDra. ) Examininq the ad-
ditional inf ornatj.on available to us at this tine, however, ve
conclude that the "public Aenerally" exception is applicable to
these sources of prornised incorne. This is so because each source
of promj.sed incomL i.s a retail nerchant within the redevelopnent
are; and presumabLy any redeveloPnent decision will affect these
6 Foa purposes of our discussion tre assune that this corporation
is engaged in retaj.l sales-


our File No. I- 9o-559a
Page 11
retail merchants in a manner which is not distinguishable fron the
effect on all retail merchants nithin the redevelopment project
area, a group which constitutes a significant segment of the
public. 
(owen, supra. )
we thus conclude that the 'tpublic generally" exception
applies to these sources of incorue to counciLmember van Deventer-
consequently, the retail merchants lrho are sources of promised
income to councilnember van Deventer are not disqualifying sources
of incone. I,Ie caution, however, that if a particular decision
will have a singular effect on one of these sources of incone
which is distinguishable fron the effect of the decision on other
retail merchants, d isqual i f ication is required if the effect wilI
be material.
Bi furcation
You have also asked whether redevelopnent decisions nay be
considered independently of each other to reduce the disqualifica-
tion of the public officials 
making the decisj.ons. Under certain
circunstance!, Iarge and complex decisions may be divided into
separate decisions when an official 
has a disqualifying interest
in one conponent of the decisions which is not interdependent upon
other components. Hotrever, under sone circumstances. a series of
decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately.
(Mil1er edvice Letter, No. A-82-11.9.) This would be the case if a
.i"cision on one aspect of a redevelopnent ptan will substantially
affect a decision on another aspect of the plan. Under such
circunstances, if an official 
:nust disqualify hinrself or herself
frorn participating in one of the decisions, he or she is also
aisquitified fron participating in the other decision.
For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark
si.tes, a decision to select one of the sites is essentialJ-y a
decision against the other autopark site. 
(see e-9. Boocraafd
Advice Letter, No. I-90-347; Miranda Advice Letter. No. I-88-373;
Miranda Advice Letter, No. I-88-253.) Sirnilarly, decisions

ANALYS I S
we have analyzed the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act which are applicable to the financial interests of Mayor
waters and councilmember van Deventer in our advice to you dated
April 15, 1991. (See Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-91-098. ) Please

we proceed nol, to deternine rrhether the additional facts
provided with your request for reconsideration would alter our
advice to you dated October 5, 1990. (See Eiser Advice Letter,
No. I-90-559) we had previously advised you that the business
tenants leasing space in the cornroercial buildings owned by uayor
waters and Councilmenber van Deventer lrere potentially
disqualifying sources of income. we had also advised you that the
business interests of Mayor waters and Councilnember van Deventer
Iocated in the redevelopment project area e/ere potentially
disquatifying. 
Upon review of the additional inforrnation you have
provided, we now conclude .that the 'tpublic generally" exception
applies to these tenants and business interests which are,
therefore, not dj.squalifying financial interests. 
Holrever, the
ttpubLic generallytr exception does not apply to the cornrnercial real
estate interests of !,layor waters and councilnember van Deventer.
Moreover, the rrpublic generally" exception does not apply to
CounciLmember zarate's interest in an apartment building.
In addition, we are now providing you with a discussion of
the concept of bifurcation of issues which nay be applicable to
the proposed decisions. This issue was not raised in your
previous request for advice.

PubIic cenera I Iv
Even rrhen the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disqua I if ication is required only if the
effect of the decision is distinguishable from the effect on the
public Aenerally. (section 87103.) If the decision does not af-
fect al.1 mernbers of the public in the sane manner, disqualifica-
tion rnay be required unless the effect of the decision is the same
as its effect on a significant segTEent of the public.
(Regulation 18703.) The Conmission has never adopted a strict
arithrnetic test for deterrnining rrhat constitutes a significant
segTnent of the public. 
However, in order for the "public
generally" exception to appJ.y, the population affected nust be
large in number and heterogeneous in nature. (In re Ferraro
(1978) 4 FPPC ops. 62; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. I-88-430.)
Councilnember Zarate
Applying this analysis to your facts, lre conclude that the
"public generalLy't exception applies to councilmember zarate's
interest in her single-family residence. This residence is
Iocated within the redevelopnent area. You have advised us that
67 percent of aII single-family residences in the city are located
in the redevelopment area. Thus, any decision affectinq the value
of Councilmember zarate's residence wiII affect a significant seg-
ment of the public in National city in substantj.ally the same man-
ner. Accordingly, the "public Aenerally" exception appli'es to
this interest in real proPerty and therefore the real property is
not a disqualifying financial interest.
conversely, CounciLmenber Zarate's interest in an apartment
building is a disqualifying financial interest. 
Although 67
percent of aII apartnent buildings in National' City are located
wittrin the redevelopnent area, the t'public generallyrr exception is
inapplicable to this interest in real Property. The commission
has recognized a very narrow exception with respect to owners of
three or fe$/er rental units. 
In Ferraro, suDra, the Comrnission
stated that a group that was Iarge in nunbers and heterogeneous in
qua)-ity constituted a significant segnent of the public for
purposes of the "public generallyrt excepti.on. Applying these
principles, the Comrnission concluded that or.rners of three or feser
residential rental units in the city of Los AngeLes constituted a
significant segment of the public.
counciLnember Zarate's building consists of more than three
units. 
Thus, as the owner of nore than three rental unj.ts, she i,s
a nember of the residential rental property business shj.ch
constitutes an industry. In order to prevent members of
homogeneous interest groups fron participating in decisions which
wiII affect their own interests, the "public generally" exception
is inapplicable to nenbers of a single industry. 
(Ferraro, $PE!! r
at pages 64-66. ) 
This interest in real property is, therefore, a
disqualifyj.ng financial interest and councilnember zarate nay not
participate in decisions which will have a rnaterial financial
effect upon the value of this apartment buiJ.ding.

.,
llavor Waters
a. Commercial Buildings
Mayor waters owns three conmercial buildings in the
redevelopnent area. There are 712 commercial and industrj,al
buildings within the redevelopnent area. Mayor waters leases

nesses with sales tax pernits operating in the city.

presurnably includes the co:nmercial office machines sales and

we had previously advised you that the business tenants in
these conmercial buildings r.rere disqualifying sources of j.ncome.
Hov/ever, you have provided us with the additional information that
78 percent of all businesses in the city are located hrithin the
redevelopnent area. For purposes of our discussion we assume that
a high percentage of these businesses are retail establishments.
Therefore, we no$, conclude that the retail merchants leasing space
in Mayor waters' conmercial buiJ.dings constitute the I'public
generally" for purposes of our a4alysis and are thus not
disqualifying sources of income.4 Note, however, that if a
particular decision will have a un.ique effect uPon one of these
tenants. d i sti,ngu ishable from the effect of the decision on other
retail merchants, disqua 1i f ication is required if the effect will
be :naterial.
In the context of redevelopment decisions, the Commission has
applied the ttpublic generally'r exception to retail businesses
located within the redevelopnent project area. (In re owen (1976)
2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In Owen, the Conmission concluded that retail
merchants within the redevelopment area constitute a najor part of
what is generally regarded as the business connunity which, taken
as a whole, may reasonably be regarded as the "Pub1j.c generally.rl
In owen, supra, the connission discussed the interests of
retail nerchants in the context of redevelopment decisions- The
Conrnission concluded that the adoption of the redeveloPnent plan
coul.d have some economic repercussions on the retail nerchants of
the city. 
However, when a substantial number and variety of
retaiL establishrnents wiLl be affected in substantially the sane
nanner, the "public generally't exception applies to these retail
nerchants.
4 wh"r, rendering ad.vice, the commission does not act as a finder
of fact, but bases its advice upon the information provi-ded by the
requester. (In re Oqlesbv (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) 
The
di.f ferent conclusion reached in this letter does not reflect 
a
change in legal interpretation, but only the application of legal
priniiples to the new facts lrhich you have provided-

we thus conclude that the 'tpublic generally" exception

Bi furcation
You have also asked whether redevelopnent decisions nay be
considered independently of each other to reduce the disqualifica-
tion of the public officials 
making the decisj.ons. Under certain
circunstance!, Iarge and complex decisions may be divided into
separate decisions when an official 
has a disqualifying interest
in one conponent of the decisions which is not interdependent upon
other components. Hotrever, under sone circumstances. a series of
decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately.
(Mil1er edvice Letter, No. A-82-11.9.) This would be the case if a
.i"cision on one aspect of a redevelopnent ptan will substantially
affect a decision on another aspect of the plan. Under such
circunstances, if an official 
:nust disqualify hinrself or herself
frorn participating in one of the decisions, he or she is also
aisquitified fron participating in the other decision.
For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark
si.tes, a decision to select one of the sites is essentialJ-y a
decision against the other autopark site. 
(see e-9. Boocraafd
Advice Letter, No. I-90-347; Miranda Advice Letter. No. I-88-373;
Miranda Advice Letter, No. I-88-253.) Sirnilarly, decisions
regaralinq the issuance of bonds or the extension of the
redevetofrnent plan to a later date may be linked to decisions
wnich migtrt affect the financial interests of the councilmenbers.
If such is the case, the decisions may not be bifurcated as one
decision rrilI have an effect on the other related decisions'
If you determine that decisions in which the councj'lmenbers
have finincial 
interests nay be segregated frorn other decisions,
once a decision in which an offi.cial has a financial interest has
been segregated and acted upon, the official 
may then . participate
as to tf,e other components of the redeveloPnent plan in which he
or she has no finantial interest. 
(KiIIian Advice Letter, No' A-
89-522; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) However, . because
of the po.tilfe 
i.,terrelationship of redevelop:nent decisions, the
,uy"i ui'ta councilnerrbers nay parlicipate in decisions regarding
arLas where they have no ec6nornic interests only if the decision
on any one compon"nt wilI not have a reasonably foreseeable
materiaL f i.nantial effect upon their economic interests or affect
other decisions. (Huffaker, supra- ) If the decision will not

have such an effect, participation is not prohibited by the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act so long as the city
council adheres to the follotring procedure:
(1) The decisions in which the nayor and the councilmembers

(2) The decisions from which the nayor and councilnembers

vote do not affect the previous decisions frorn which they are

we trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
